SUMMARY:
This document provides a historical overview of the development of world literature from ancient civilizations to the present day.

FULL CONTENT:
![SlideShare a Scribd company logo](https://public.slidesharecdn.com/images/next/svg/logo/slideshare-scribd-company.svg?w=256&q=75)

# WORLD LITERATURE

![Andre Philip Tacderas, profile picture](https://cdn.slidesharecdn.com/profile-photo-100002406493412-48x48.jpg?cb=1754805133)

This document provides a historical overview of the development of world literature from ancient civilizations to the present day. It traces the evolution of literary styles and genres across eras and regions, highlighting influential works that shaped each period. Examples of famous writers from different parts of the world are also listed, demonstrating literature's role in conveying diverse cultural perspectives over time.

![Literature of
the World
21st Century Literature
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-1-320.jpg)
![What is World
Literature?
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-2-320.jpg)
![Literature of
the World
World literature
refers to writings and
stories that come
from various parts of
the world,
representing
different cultures,
perspectives, and
experiences.
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-3-320.jpg)
![Literature is a powerful
means of conveying ideas,
emotions, and cultural
perspectives. It plays a
significant role in shaping
societies and connecting
people across time and
place.
World Literature
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-4-320.jpg)
![Historical Roots of Literature
Ancient Civilization
1. Mesopotamia (circa 3500 BCE)
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-5-320.jpg)
![Ancient Civilization
1. Mesopotamia (circa 3500 BCE)
Cuneiform Writing: The
Sumerians in Mesopotamia
developed cuneiform script,
one of the earliest known
writing systems. Early writings
on clay tablets included epic
tales like the "Epic of
Gilgamesh," providing insights
into their beliefs and values.
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-6-320.jpg)
![Historical Roots of Literature
Ancient Civilization
2. Ancient Egypt (circa 3100 BCE):
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-7-320.jpg)
![Ancient Civilization
2. Ancient Egypt (circa 3100 BCE):
Hieroglyphics: Egyptians
used hieroglyphics to record
religious texts, administrative
information, and stories. The
"Book of the Dead" is a notable
example, reflecting beliefs
about the afterlife.
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-8-320.jpg)
![Historical Roots of Literature
Classic Antiquity:
Ancient Greece (8th to 4th century BCE):
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-9-320.jpg)
![Classic Antiquity
Ancient Greece (8th to 4th century BCE):
Epics and Drama: Homer's
"Iliad" and "Odyssey" set the stage
for Greek literature. Tragedies and
comedies by playwrights like
Sophocles and Aristophanes
became central to cultural
expression.
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-10-320.jpg)
![Historical Roots of Literature
Classic Antiquity:
Ancient Rome (3rd century BCE to 5th century CE):
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-11-320.jpg)
![Classic Antiquity
Ancient Rome (3rd century
BCE to 5th century CE):
Latin Literature: Virgil's
"Aeneid" and Cicero's
philosophical works were
influential. The Roman Empire's
decline saw a shift to early
Christian literature.
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-12-320.jpg)
![Historical Roots of Literature
Medieval Literature (5th to 15th century):
Early Christian Literature (3rd to 7th century):
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-13-320.jpg)
![Medieval Literature
Early Christian Literature
(3rd to 7th century):
Religious Texts: Writings by
early Christian theologians, like
St. Augustine's "Confessions,"
combined religious and
philosophical themes.
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-14-320.jpg)
![Historical Roots of Literature
Medieval Literature (5th to 15th century):
Islamic Golden Age (8th to 14th century):
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-15-320.jpg)
![Medieval Literature
Islamic Golden Age (8th to
14th century):
Arabic Literature: The
"Thousand and One Nights"
showcased Arabic storytelling.
Scholars translated Greek and
Roman works, preserving
classical knowledge.
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-16-320.jpg)
![Historical Roots of Literature
Renaissance and Early Modern Period
(14th to 18th century):
Italian Renaissance (14th to 17th century):
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-17-320.jpg)
![Renaissance and Early Modern Period
Italian Renaissance (14th to
17th century):
Humanism: Literary works
emphasized human experience,
with Dante's "Divine Comedy" as
a pinnacle. Petrarch's sonnets
influenced European poetry.
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-18-320.jpg)
![Historical Roots of Literature
Renaissance and Early Modern Period
(14th to 18th century):
Elizabethan England (16th century):
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-19-320.jpg)
![Renaissance and Early Modern Period
Elizabethan England (16th
century):
Shakespearean Plays:
William Shakespeare's plays,
including "Romeo and Juliet" and
"Hamlet," are iconic examples of
Elizabethan drama.
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-20-320.jpg)
![Historical Roots of Literature
Enlightenment and Romanticism (18th to 19th century):
Age of Enlightenment (17th to 18th century):
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-21-320.jpg)
![Enlightenment and Romanticism
Age of Enlightenment (17th
to 18th century):
Philosophical Essays:
Enlightenment thinkers like
Voltaire and Rousseau used
literature to explore reason,
liberty, and social progress.
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-22-320.jpg)
![Historical Roots of Literature
Enlightenment and Romanticism (18th to 19th century):
Romantic Movement (late 18th to early 19th century):
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-23-320.jpg)
![Enlightenment and Romanticism
Romantic Movement (late
18th to early 19th century):
Emotion and Nature:
Romantic poets like Wordsworth
and Shelley emphasized
emotion, individualism, and a
connection to nature.
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-24-320.jpg)

Post-World War I (20th century):
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-25-320.jpg)
![Modern and Contemporary Literature
Post-World War I (20th
century):
Modernist Movement:
Writers like James Joyce and
Virginia Woolf experimented
with narrative forms, reflecting
the fragmented post-war reality.
 ](https://image.slidesharecdn.com/21stcenturyoftheworld-231121123645-fc7093b9/85/WORLD-LITERATURE-26-320.jpg)

Modern and Contemporary Literature (20th century to

Postcolonial Literature
(mid-20th century to
present):
Cultural Identity: Authors
from former colonies, like
Chinua Achebe and Gabriel
Garcia Marquez, explored
postcolonial themes and cultural

enriched_points.expanded_content: Literature has evolved from ancient epics, like the 'Epic of Gilgamesh', through medieval narratives, to modernist works, reflecting societal changes and cultural developments over centuries.
Literary movements such as Romanticism, Realism, and Modernism shaped global literature, introducing new styles and themes, influencing writers across generations and cultures.
Notable authors, including Shakespeare, Tolstoy, and Achebe, contributed significantly to literature, exploring diverse themes, styles, and cultural narratives that resonate globally.
